content.problemStatement:
Chef is hosting a party. In preparation, he has laid out NNN glasses of juice on a table, all in a line.

The iii-th glass from the left initially has AiA_iAi‚Äã units of juice.

For aesthetic reasons, Chef would really like all the glasses to have an equal amount of juice.

To achieve this, he can do the following:


Choose an index iii (1‚â§i‚â§N1 \leq i \leq N1‚â§i‚â§N).
Choose two **non-negative integers** xxx and yyy such that x+y‚â§Aix+y \leq A_ix+y‚â§Ai‚Äã.
Then,

If i>1i \gt 1i>1, pour xxx units of juice into glass i‚àí1i-1i‚àí1, i.e. increase Ai‚àí1A_{i-1}Ai‚àí1‚Äã by xxx.
If i<Ni \lt Ni<N, pour yyy units of juice into glass i+1i+1i+1, i.e. increase Ai+1A_{i+1}Ai+1‚Äã by yyy.
Remove glass iii from the table. This reduces NNN by 111.
Re-index the remaining glasses to start from 111, left-to-right.


Find the **minimum** number of such operations that Chef needs, to ensure that all remaining glasses have an equal amount of juice remaining.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN ‚Äî the initial number of glasses.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,‚Ä¶,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1‚Äã,A2‚Äã,‚Ä¶,AN‚Äã ‚Äî the initial amounts of juice in the glasses.




Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: the minimum number of operations needed to make all the glasses have an equal amount of juice.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 500050005000.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Choose i=2i = 2i=2, x=3x = 3x=3, y=2y = 2y=2 (which is valid because x+y‚â§A2=6x+y \leq A_2 = 6x+y‚â§A2‚Äã=6).
This corresponds to pouring 333 units into the left glass and 222 units into the right one, and then removing the middle glass.
After this, the glasses have quantities [4,4][4, 4][4,4], so we're done.
Test case 222: All the glasses have equal quantities already, no moves are needed.
Test case 333: One optimal sequence of operations is as follows.

Choose i=3,x=1,y=0i = 3, x = 1, y = 0i=3,x=1,y=0. The glasses are now [4,4,1,2][4, 4, 1, 2][4,4,1,2].
Choose i=3,x=0,y=1i = 3, x = 0, y = 1i=3,x=0,y=1. The glasses are now [4,4,3][4, 4, 3][4,4,3].
Choose i=2,x=1,y=2i = 2, x = 1, y = 2i=2,x=1,y=2. The glasses are now [5,5][5, 5][5,5], and we're done.

It can be shown that using two or fewer moves is not possible.
